speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: It were. Talk slowly.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said close the door for the noise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah, OK. So I-- so I'm not allowed to stay here, Lydia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let her stay here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Either in-- no. Either in the bedroom--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't mind. She's going to be quiet now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'm going to be quiet, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a word you can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. [NON-ENGLISH]. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. No? [NON-ENGLISH]. Go in there in that-- into your bedroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you have some reading to do in the bedroom?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. OK. I'm going. I'm going to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] I'm abandoning her.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] I know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] We've had beautiful weather.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Not yet. Lately? The first two--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: My vacation was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January the 22, 1996. The survivor is Bella Sobol. The interviewer is Bonnie Slavin. The city and state, Miami Beach, Florida; the country, United States of America. The language is English. This is tape one. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Today is January 22, 1996. My name is Bonnie Slavin, S-L-A-V-I-N. I am proud to be conducting an interview with Mrs. Bella Sobol. This is Miami Beach, Florida, United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on one second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bella Sobol.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-L-A S-O-B-O-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plonsk, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: March the 21st, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kozica.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. K-O-Z-I-C-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Mrs. Sobol, please describe what your life was like when you were growing up, before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was really-- I had a nice family consisting of three-- four sisters and a brother, a father, and a mother, and-- around the family. I was a happy-- a happy child. I went to school. And I had my friends. We was, you know, always, you know, doing things, you know, what we're supposed-- like children supposed to do. And you know, in-- in-- in-- and in school, it was very, you know, we was very active. And the time, you know, between the time, I belonged to an organization. When I was 11-years-old, I belonged to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, you know, we-- we came in. We was dancing. We was singing. We was learning a little Hebrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got-- all day we went on outings, just like the Girl Scouts and the Boy Scouts. We went to other cities. And we, you know, we met people from all over, you know, girls and boys from all over the United States. And this happens in the winter-- one time in the winter and one time in the summer. Till, you know this was going on, you know, my happy life with my-- with my family, with a beautiful family. It was going on till 1939. When the war broke out, I was 14-years-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back. What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I finished seven grades and nothing more. In between, I was learning a little Jewish. I had special classes-- not in the school-- private-- Hebrew and a little in Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a businessman. He was, you know, in livestocks. And he was constantly traveling with his-- with his-- with the merchandise to Warsaw. And he would-- had his place. That's where he was selling, you know. And came the weekend, he came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Shabbat-- the Shabbat was always with the father and the mother. And the grandmother was always invited, you know, to-- to-- to the-- to the Shabbat. And, you know, this was-- everything was beautiful life-- a beautiful life before, you know, before the war starts. In your city, how large was the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- it was a nice Jewish community-- 10,000 Jews-- yeah, all kind, you know, rich and poor, and-- and all kind of people. It was, you know, very nice. There was a-- there was a Hebrew school. And we had a gymnasium in the school. It was, you know, a really cultured city with a lot of organizations, all kind organizations-- Jewish. You know, the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] and then the-- and the-- and the Betar, and all-- and the labor movement-- everything was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, did you ever hear your family speak about Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We speak-- we knew something was not kosher, because before, in 1938, when we saw Polish people were standing by Jewish stores with-- how you say that-- written down, don't go in in the-- don't buy Jewish stuff. Don't go. And we-- you know-- when you're young, you don't even think, you know, something's going to happen to you, or something's going to happen in the world. So, you know, we heard stories. And people start coming from Germany. Now I can remember, to mine aunt, to my neighbors, family came-- came with empty at night. They came with just the nightgowns and their housecoats. And they start telling stories, you know. There is a Germany and that-- and they're sending out the Jews. And we didn't really-- we-- I-- not me. I didn't really comprehend what's going on there. Till, you know, till 1939, the war broke out, and so everybody said, oh enough-- not another day-- two in a month. Who's going to, you know-- Hitler's going to take over Poland. And the thing is, you know, it happened this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, did you ever experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You personally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I went to school. It was just Jewish people. There was extra one for the-- for the Gentiles, and one for the-- for the Jewish people. Because it was a big population. So-- so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they separated. This was a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: A public school, yeah. There was so many children, they had to build a school. So they built a school for the Jewish children. There was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was separated, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, did you have a Jewish education at that Jewish public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They didn't-- they didn't teach Jewish. That was a public school just to learn the language and the things, what, you know, you learn in a public school-- nothing to do-- we had just Jewish religion. Once-- one hour a week, they teach us Jewish religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a difference between the two schools-- the Polish school and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I cannot tell. I cannot tell, probably. Because whoever went-- there was plenty kids between ours-- in my school-- who started in the Polish school. So even there, the Jews went in the morning, and the Poles went in the afternoon. There was always separated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family-- hearing some of these stories about what was going on in Germany before the war-- did they ever think about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. First of all, they didn't have-- they didn't have no family. So there was no even thinking about we're going to go to the United States, because we didn't have nobody here.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not leaving Poland, not trying to get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no-- no place, no. They-- nobody had in mind, you know, something's going to terrible happen. You have to leave your home and go someplace. OK, people moved out. People emigrated. They had, you know, relatives, they had children, or they had parents. They went. On-- in our case, we didn't have nobody. And our place was to stay there with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened September 1, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- December-- at the-- two days before, they start mobilizing, you know, whoever was, you know, the year they was born, you know. They took to the-- the military.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was mobilizing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Poland. Poland start mobilizing the country. I had already-- at this time, I had two brother-in-laws. So that was already, I think, over-- over the year. And we were sleeping. There was, you know, there was no newspapers. In the town, there was a big billboard. And they put up the notices-- who has to come and what's going to happen. So everybody who was born in this and this year, had to go, you know, and-- and become a soldier. And this was a couple of days before they started. And in-- in the-- the-- and September the 1st, just like today, I remember I was in the-- in the house. And we heard planes. We heard boom. And, you know, the town, it was not so big. There was one bridge-- one bridge to go through. And right away they said the bridge got blown up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then everybody was screaming. Oh, this is our planes, our planes-- this Polish planes. German planes? And they said, we are-- we going to beat them. And believe-- there's nothing happened-- happened. The thing is, after one bombing, the whole town-- had-- it was not ruined. I mean, the most vital places, like the train station and the-- and the bridge.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two days later-- yeah-- in the same evening-- the same evening, I saw a lot of soldiers-- Polish-- with artillery, with old, you know, everything was on horses, the soldiers on horses with wagons going to the front. They had to go through mine town to go to the front. And, you know, and-- and people start, you know, people was very afraid what's going to happen. So everybody-- so the next day, whole families start running. They run to Warsaw-- I, my sisters, between them, my two sisters took the children. And my father was there, my sister, my other sister, who was not married, she was-- she was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One, it was-- my oldest was Frida. And my-- mine second one was Esther. And my third, who is alive, is Hannah-- Hanka-- Hannah. And my brother was Abe. So my brother and me, we stayed with our mother. And the other-- the other family, all of them went to Warsaw. There was unbelievable what was going on at the roads. People didn't have transportation. Everybody was walking-- walking because they said they're not going to bomb Warsaw. They're going to, you know-- they're going to-- they're not-- the soldiers, they have a big fortress there. And they're going to fight, and not going  to let him in. This-- this whole episode took four weeks-- took them four weeks till the-- the soldiers, you know, the Germans took over Warsaw. And there was-- this, I can't-- I-- I was not there. So I cannot tell. The only thing I know, Warsaw was bombed terrible. Because when they came back-- they came back, you know, after four or five weeks. I remember between there was the high holy days. And, you know, it was terrible in the house-- no-- nothing to eat. The other way, all the-- all the stores in the-- in the town-- in the city-- it was roped from-- from-- the Polish people broke in in all the Jewish stores. And they took out everything, you know. There was no money. There was no food. There was nothing, you know. I don't know how we survived or what the mother did. I really don't-- cannot tell. I tried to go and bring something myself, you know. I went to the store, you know, whatever they-- the Polish people left potatoes. We picked it up, and we brought home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the family came back-- and this was a very-- a tragic coming back. Because there was still bombing, and there was still people who was trying to come back. The German catch them. And there was-- they're looking for the papers, where they're going. Nobody had passports. Nobody know nothing what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, they-- everybody came home. And the tragedy started. Nope, there was no money. There was no work. We were still living in our own house. This was 1939 till 1940, I think. 1940, in the spring, they said they're going to put all the Jews in to one place. And the town, it was not big. The-- the-- they picked out the most-- the-- the-- the worst part of the town-- the poorest part of the town. There was no streets. There was just stables from horses. And they said all the Jews are going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the place where we lived, we had to move out, take our belongings, whatever we still had there-- the little furnitures or things like that. And we went on a place-- no-- no house. They didn't give us nothing. So I had an aunt who had-- how you say this-- a little place on a field, you know. She was holding the-- I don't know-- something. She said, go in there. You're going to-- you know, it's summer. It was summer. You're going to stay in this place till we going to find something. What was it like? It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: A shack. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- it was a little shack just-- and we made our bed there. We couldn't take the furniture, so we took a piece of plywood. We made a bed. So there was-- the other sisters, you know, they was married already the others. So it was just me, and my sister Hanka, and my brother, my father, and my mother. And it was-- and we were staying a whole summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We didn't do-- there was-- I did. They took first of all, the Germans, you know, they made-- when they made the ghetto-- but even before, right away they took the Jews for them to work-- the young girls, like me, you know, all the-- the old ones, the young ones. The intelligentsia, they took the other way-- way. They put them on trucks. They took them to jails. Or they took them to another camp. They took them to [NON-ENGLISH] camps, to working camps. And these people never came back. And we never saw them. Some went to jail. Behind the town, there was a jail. So they took them-- some to jail. Or people who didn't have money, they said, you're going to give so much-- so much money, we're not going to take you. Whoever didn't have the money, they took them to jail. And so the young ones, they took to work. So mine job was I worked on the police station. I washed floors. I cleaned toilets. I did everything. I was young, and I was-- and I still, you know, believed I'm going to-- I'm going to be alive. They cannot do nothing to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I worked every day from the beginning, you know. They assigned me. Yeah. In the ghetto was a committee. And this committee had to assign. The Germans, they send in, they need so many, many people. And the-- and the committee was called Judenrat. And they had to assign the people to every job what they're told them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my job was to go and clean the, you know, to wash the floors-- wash the floors, clean, wipe the windows-- anything. So we still found someplace we got a piece of bread from, you know, from the policemen, from the German. And the one that-- you know-- another time, I went, you know-- this was in the-- late in the fall-- we went on the fields. And we had to dig the potatoes. We was working for a few miles, you know? And we dig the potatoes. We couldn't take the potatoes home with us. This was all-- everything for them. Nice to put them together in bags and put them on-- on-- on trucks, and things like. So this was the consistence, the time when I was in the ghetto, you know, between, you know-- I-- we saw to do some things, you know. There was hanging people. The people who ran away, outside the ghetto they found people who were smuggling, bringing in stuff, you know, that they brought them in back to the-- to the ghetto. Could you leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I went a few times myself out. I was smuggling, I-- you know. I tried to buy bread. Because I was thinking, you know, I'm a girl. A man was, you know, he was afraid to go out. And I knew already those-- where to go. So I smuggled myself out. And I went. I had a Polish bakery. They knew me very well. And there was a young man. He said always, Bella, you going to come. I'm going to give you bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I always ran in the back of the-- of the city. And I had to go on the other side. And early in the morning, and I always brought-- came back with some bread. We came back with the bread, and, you know, in the other times, you know, I went out. And I got some other stuff. So it was a little-- What-- how did the ghetto look? Did it have a-- a fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There-- there-- no. There was no fence. There was just-- one side where we went in, there was-- how you say this? You're closing up-- a big-- not a door, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: A gate?

speaker: interviewee
text: A gate. There was one side a gate, and then the ghetto from the other side. All the buildings where the ghetto was, it was like one big street-- a street and little streets. So there was one side. On this street, there was living people who was Jewish people. And on the other side-- the other side, there was, in the front, a few houses. And in the back it was a field with stables. And there are people. In the winter time, they-- they had to stay there. So when somebody wanted to go down from one side to another, he had to go through the gate. And there was a policeman standing, a Jewish policeman. And you had-- you could go in. Oh, there was-- like my case, I wanted to go out from the ghetto. I knew the-- the roads from the other side through the fields. The fields was not closed up. And I ran through the fields. And I went on the Polish-- on the Polish side. So there is where I was always in contact. A few times I was lucky. I went out, and I came back. And I brought the food. So the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your ghetto have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no name. There was Plonsk, you know, Plonsk ghetto. And through the ghetto, they're starting-- you know, this was 1940. They closed up the ghetto. And they start bringing from little towns. There was-- we were surrounded by very little towns. So they had to close up completely the towns, and they brought in the people to us-- to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you can imagine. There was-- even we didn't have where to stay. So what happened to these people? People were sleeping in the synagogues. People were sleeping any place where there was just a place where to go in and put down their head. People was living miserable, miserable life-- sickness, typhus was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And between-- and this was-- now I'm going to go to 1941. 1941, they had made-- the Germans came in with big machines. They said all the ghetto is going to get [NON-ENGLISH]-- how you say-- clean up, you know, disinfected. They're going to disinfect. They took to one shul. We had a big shul. We had a small shul. And they converted the shul to a-- to a bath. They brought the machines. And they brought, you know, the-- they took people from the ghetto. I was working there, so-- so I-- so I know what was going on. Everybody by-- by the house. They brought in. They shaved them, They disinfected them, you know, because they said, oh, everybody had lice. This is 100% sure. And-- and they was afraid, because typhus started, you know, the epidemics that came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember like today, they had combs-- combs. I-- there was the combs like from-- not from the material that we make. It was a special metal-- very hard-- very, very hard. And these combs, when you put in your head, they took out the lice. So when somebody came in there, when they had lice, you had to cut their hair. So the people who I knew, before they went in, I gave them the comb. And they combed out. Oh, I brought it home. I stole it at night, because at night, they didn't work. And I'd give the people the combs. And they combed their hair clean-- completely clean. All the lice, everything came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came next day, it was their turn to come in, you know, got that-- to got-- they went through, because they didn't have-- they didn't had lice. So this was going on for weeks till all the many thousands of people to had, you know. And-- and still, the typhus came. And there was a-- they converted one little house. They made a hospital. There was a few nurses. I don't remember. There was-- there was some doctors. I don't remember the doctors stayed with us, or they ran away when-- you know, when the war broke out. People died. And I was myself infected. Me and my mother, we stayed in one bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just maybe a week-- maybe a few days I was staying there in the place. Because I was-- I had a very light type of typhus. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone take care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some, you know, some people, you know, the nurses. There was-- I don't know that-- because we was sleeping. At this-- I remember we were sleeping together in one bed. There was, you know, so many people. It was-- it filled up the whole little house. It was filled up with people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came out, you know, we came out. And we came back, you know, to our shack. So when the winter start, the winter, so how could we stay there? So my father had a brother. And they had their-- their house. They was staying in the ghetto. So he said-- he had-- his house consisted of two rooms. So like this, we was just four people. He took us in. So the kitchen, we put our bed here. We didn't have nothing. We took our-- his bed. And he had another bed. And he had a family of three-- two son-- had two daughters and a son. He took us in. So we was happy we had a place where-- we didn't have where to sleep. Me and Hanka slept on a table-- a table-- a regular table. And on the side by the table, we put a barrel, because the table was not long enough for us. So we put the barrels up. It could hold our feet. I have to remind Hanka about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] So-- and, you know, and you know, we got the food. We got rations-- something they gave us to eat-- just rations-- a piece of bread, you know. No coals, nothing, so we was burning our furnitures-- this is what we took out from the house. Slowly, we-- we burned the beds. We burned the closets. We burned anything what we could put our hands on. We burned. We was lucky. On the yard, where we was living, was a bakery. And this bakery was baking bread for the people-- for the ghetto. So we always run in. And we warmed ourselves up. There was a young man. And this young man was stealing a piece of dough at night. He was stealing a piece of dough. And he was making little-- little breads.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at night, we were sleeping by the window. And it was in the summer. It was hot. The window was open. Who was up late? Who was going to steal me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he brought us the little breads. I-- really, I would be very happy to remember his name. I don't remember. He was a very-- it was a-- this man, he was, you know, not from my hometown. He was the one who came from another place. And I don't remember his name. He-- I looked for him. I looked for him in-- afterwards-- I mean, in the concentration. And I-- I saw him once. I never spoke to him. I never-- and I just saw him going by. From far away, I recognized him. I'm going to come later back to this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was going on, you know, with the-- in the ghetto, you know? We-- was going on terrible things. You know, there was a lot of children that lost their parents. And the children-- it was terrible-- just to look on them, you know, walking around, begging for food-- begging for food, the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, we tried. You know, we really tried our best what we could, you know, to survive. Yeah, and I was-- I started working again. This time I worked like a dressmaker. Because before a year, before the war-- a year before the war, I finished school. So I went to learn a trade. So I went to make, you know, how to sew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the ghetto, I-- I went to work for a-- for a lady who was working for the-- for the Germans. And, you know, we were sewing, things like that. So I made a few dollars, you know, and things. So this was going on the whole time till I-- till they took us out. As the restrictions came more and more, and you had less and less food, what other-- where was your sisters-- your two married sisters at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was there. They was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was in the ghetto-- both of them. One husband-- my youngest sister-- was a tailor. And he was working for-- for the Germans. He was-- he was a very good tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and they-- they came in. They took one big building, and they converted it. Go ahead. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: They converted it for all trades. So there were shoes, this and that. There was-- there was tailors. And there was jewelry men, watch makers, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So mine-- my brother-in-law, he was-- he was the lucky one. He worked there, too. And he was making suits.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in, you know, they wanted suits. So he had to supply. They didn't supply the material. You had to supply the material. You had to supply everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he got some, a little more food. So the sister, you know, had-- and my other-- my other brother-in-law, he was a butcher. So he was-- yeah. Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.
